content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. See the movie 'They Live'

**Score:** 100
**Body:** I don't know if my post will even be allowed or not but if you are curious and want to know what exactly is going on nowadays you have to watch 'They Live' 

It is the documentary of what the €lite are doing to us and now if you are consuming or reading about any content related to €p$tein files then STOP IMMEDIATELY this is a setup by the gov to make us mentally weak and lower our frequencies so that this will be normalised...

Go watch this movie and then STOP consuming these type of content for a few days and start to meditate because they are trying to distract us and make us weak as now the frequency of world is high which is not good for the €lite Class as they have secrets and if we want to resist and fight them we have to be mentally prepared and strong 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Look, I love They Live and agree with your recommendation and its reasoning, but your proposed solution of remaining ignorant to the world goings-on and just meditating is not the way.
  - In fact, that is the exact opposite of the message of They Live, which is that all of us — even a random nobody like Nada — should stand up and fight. The whole famous “put on the glasses” fight scene is literally about NOT just ignoring the problem.
  - They Live is an amazing movie. But yes, sticking your head in the sand is not the way forward. Also, getting your information from tiktoks that play up the most heinous accusations with horror music in the background isn't helping anyone if their algorithm keeps feeding it to them.

OP, please try to refrain from watching that type of content in that exact format because it is literally the same shit as the movie: "ghouls" feeding you consumerism except you're paying with engagement views and personal data.

It's affected my sleep because of the annoying sound effects and way it's presented. But I also don't watch horror movies because of those same reasons.
2. Its a funny movie, Rowdy Roddy Piper's best film--R.I.P.
  - Love the fight scene with Keith David.
    - It goes on for so long it actually starts getting funny.
3. no starting riots to cover up fraud is designed to make us weak. Watch They live because it’s awesome. 
4. He knows..... [https://i.ytimg.com/vi/nq09lywyulg/hqdefault.jpg](https://i.ytimg.com/vi/nq09lywyulg/hqdefault.jpg)
5. OBEY
6. WTF is 'our frequencies' and what does that mean?  Nah I don't think that putting our heads in the sand over any of the Epstein stuff and good vibing is gonna right this ship.  

## 2. Movies that keep you in a trance from start to finish

**Score:** 21
**Body:** I just watched Apocalypse Now Final Cut on 4K for the first ever time and my god it was an incredible experience, it had a 3 hour runtime and not once did I feel it drag or lose attention. I entered a trance from the moment it started and flowed with the entire film taking everything in and feeling like I was right there inside the film with the characters.

These are my favourite kind of experiences with films and need some more that offer similar experiences, 2001 A Space Odyssey is another film I remember having this same experience with. I guess they are called cinema classics for a reason! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Apocalypto
2. Enter the Void by Gaspar Noé
3. The Thin Red Line and 
In the Mood for Love
4. 'A Clockwork Orange' (1971) never lets up. Even on rewatches I'm transfixed and horrified for the entire film.. 

The same can be said for 'Goodfellas' (1990) too.
5. Gravity (2013) is a very immersive experience
6. Poor Things
7. One Battle After Another
  - I’m not sure why this movie was a bit boring to me.
8. Boyhood (2014)

Love Exposure (2008)

Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World (2003)
9. Lawrence of Arabia. It might be the most beautiful movie ever filmed. 

## 3. Simple and peaceful.

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Hi, I'm looking for (non animation) movies which are slow moving, seemingly mundane, and have a peaceful tone - scenic is a plus. 

Examples (and recommendations) are Janet Planet - Annie Baker, Still Walking - Hirokazu Koreeda, Life of Pi - Ang Lee and Little Forrest - Yim Soon-rye. 

I've watched most of the movies which have been recommended as similar to these listed, so I'm looking for lesser known gems, thanks!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Straight Story
2. A Scene at the Sea

Columbus (2017)

Perfect Days

First Cow 
  - Thanks for these - Perfect Days looks like it will hit the spot. 
    - it will. seconding Perfect Days
3. Lost in Translation
4. Train Dreams 
5. The Substance.
Wait, no.
6. Adrift in Tokyo (Tenten) (Satoshi Miki, 2007).

Someone already recommended Perfect Days (Wim Wenders, 2023), but I'll concur.
7. Local Hero
8. Check out Paris, Texas

## 4. The Descent...messed me up more than I expected

**Score:** 17
**Body:** Watched The Descent recently and yeah… this one creeps up on you. Super tense, heavy atmosphere, and it just keeps tightening the screws.

If you like horror that’s more about pressure and dread than jumpscares, this is an easy recommend. Not a background movie at all.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Watch the UK ending, not the American one. The US version cuts the last 30 seconds and it changes everything about what the movie means.
2. The sequel wasn't bad either.
  - Yeahh
3. Which one?
4. 'Hereditary' messed me up.
5. It really screws with you, especially if you are claustrophobic. Even when nothing happens, you just expect things to go sideways any second. 
6. I'm claustrophobic so this movie had me wound up and on the edge of my seat. I loved it and even picked up a used DVD copy of it.
  - Yeah mee too
7. If you want another film that hits some similar notes I recommend As Above, So Below.

It's about an archaeologist/anthropologist documenting her search for the Philosopher's Stone, a hunt that leads her into the Parisian Catacombs. 

It's basically Tomb Raider meets Blair Witch meets The Descent and it's great. Invokes a lot of the same phobias as both Blair Witch and The Descent, and has a constant sense of growing and building dread throughout.
8. I am still bummed they didn't do it as the book. It's a great read, and covers how the Hadals have shown up in history. They have a culture/tribes, religion, whole deal. It gets very Aliens/Avatar towards the end when the megacorps show up, and the colonization metaphor gets heavy handed. 

I was very excited for the movie then pretty bummed when it was just a creature feature.

Good movie though!

## 5. Hilarious feel-good comfort movies like PB falcon, fundamentals of caregiving, & little miss sunshine?

**Score:** 15
**Body:** Going thru a rough patch in life. These funny feel good movies are getting me thru the lonely nights. 

Looking for the same vibe as …

Peanut butter falcon

Hunt for the wilder people 

Shit house 

Little miss sunshine 

Fundamentals of caregiving 

Shrinking (even tho that’s a show)

I’ve watched the way way back but didn’t find it nearly as funny as the others. Same with kings of summer. 

Also can’t tell if captain fantastic is gonna be funny enough for my taste? 

Overall, my fav on the list is fundamentals of caregiving. I loved the humor and story. 

All recs greatly appreciated! 

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Instant Family (2016)

~~Meet~~ We're the Millers (2013)

Miss Congeniality (2000)

*\*Edited to fix title*
  - Do you mean were the millers or is there another movie I’m missing? LOVE that movie. Iconic
    - Yeah, my bad \*off to correct\*
2. A League of Their Own

Zoolander 

Mystery Men
  - lol Zoolander gets me every time
3.  Bringing Up Baby, old school Cary Grant is fun. The classics are worth checking out - the dialogue is great.
4. Sunshine cleaning 


The rocker 


Paper towns


Real steel 


The way way back
5. Miss Stevens (2016) is so underrated!

Also I LOVE shithouse- if you haven’t already you should watch his other movie Cha Cha Real Smooth… one more thing lol if you can find it it just got released for some streaming I think but the tv series Hal & Harper has him in it and is amazing
  - I loved cha cha too!! Didn’t include it in this list cuz I didn’t know want to steer too much in the romance directions, but LOVED that one too. I love Cooper raiff. His show, Hal & Harper, is a must watch too!!
6. Champions (2023) - a disgraced minor-league basketball coach is sentenced to community service coaching a team of players with intellectual disabilities

I Used to Be Famous (2022) - A former boy band star unexpectedly gets a second shot at success when he forms a bond with a gifted young drummer

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Totally loved Sam Rockwell in Jojo Rabbit. His relationship with Jojo was truly great, and his character did something truly remarkable in the end.

**Score:** 1014

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. They are play it in such a fun subversion way. 

Like Rockwell being gay with Alfie Allan is played for light laughs in the beginning, but then you realize later that they take that quirky joke and make it part of a tragic realization for a character and subtle explanation as to why he has internalized his self destruction and hatred.
  - The whole movie is like this if you really look at it. So many things are played for jokes because it's through the eyes of a child that doesn't fully comprehend what's going on. Then as the movie progresses he starts to notice what's actually going on and it takes that more serious tone.
2. I love the portrayal of the Nazis in this movie. Absurd and terrifying simultaneously. Rockwell was great but Scarlett Johanson stole the show in my opinion and then broke my heart. I love this movie.
  - And it caught a lot of heat bc people said it humanized Nazis and Hitler. It made them look ridiculous imo
    - Well those Nazis were human, despicable humans but human nevertheless. This is equally true of Hitler. A bitter delusional hate filled human who got off lightly by his own hand.
    - Really people said that?? Wow that’s crazy. Literally the whole point was that Jojo actually knows nothing about Hitler (he imagines Hitler as eating meat and smoking when he famously did neither) or the Jewish people and that he was projecting the propaganda and his own imaginings onto both. I felt like movie was very clear in portraying the Nazis as evil.
3. Sam Rockwell is great in everything.
  - He is Mr. Right
4. Truly
5. Yeah I really enjoyed that movie. It was surprisingly historically accurate too I thought, despite being an absurdist comedy. 

The character mentioned in the OP was very well rendered, as it portrayed the character as not simply a one dimensional Nazi villain but someone with nuance and complexity.

Some of the lines were hilarious too, like when the kids are fighting in the battle of Berlin and one says to the other, I’m beginning to think we picked the wrong side in this one lol

## 2. Do the Oscars feel as big as they used to?

**Score:** 981
**Body:** Lately I’ve been wondering if the Oscars still hit the same way.

Best Picture used to feel like the movie everyone talked about for years. For me at least. Now sometimes the winner comes and goes and not many people seem to care. Like we went from Shindler’s List winning Best Picture to Emilia Perez getting nominated?! Or sinners breaking the record for the most nominations…

No offence to any of the winners or nominees, just wanna know what happened. Or I’m just imagining things and it was always like this. Or it’s because of the film industry and not the academy. Idk

Do the Oscars still feel important to you, or not really anymore? 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Not even close.  The idea of a celebrity was more intriguing than the actual celebrity.  The more you know about their thoughts, the less interesting they are.
  - We’ve always said “never meet your heroes”. Then we created an online world where all we see is the interior lives of stars and celebrities and it is absolutely atrocious. 

The modern world lacks all mystique that we once had.
2. Do the movies feel as big they used to? I think the answer is ‘no’
  - Yes, movies have grown less important in the general population. They don't have the mass impact they once had in the previous decades.  Films are becoming a niche thing more and more, it seems.
  - 2020 brought a lot of reality to the theaters.
3. Total joke in my eyes after learning that they didn't have to watch the films they were voting on to give awards to.
  - I remember hearing something about how Adrien Brody (The Brutalist) winning over Ralph Fiennes (Conclave) was partially because some Academy members thought that Fiennes had already won an Oscar while Brody hadn't. 

They were dead wrong: Brody was the one with an Oscar for The Pianist while Fiennes had only been nominated several times.  Seriously ridiculous. 
  - Makes you think what the point even is of them having those coveted roles

Edit: typo
4. Celebrities had a lot less exposure back in the day so getting a bunch of them together in a room and putting the event on tv was exciting.
5. The industry is wildly different and so is the Academy. The show is clearly much different. It used to be prom for famous people. Now it’s closer to just niche programming. 

Monoculture and access shifts have absolutely changed the show. This year will be more memorable than the last bunch of years, though.

## 3. Best statistics movie?

**Score:** 244

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I can watch Moneyball forever. It’s so good that I read the book (which have so more statistics and haven’t great actors)
  - It definitely has a lot of replay value
    - It's one of my favorite movies of all time I always rewatch it a few times a year
      - Me too. No idea why, feels like a comfort blanket to me.
2. Honestly, as. Brit with limited baseball knowledge most of this misses me.

The Big Short... amazing. Complex things explained in easy and entertaining ways in an amazing film.
  - I don't think you need much Baseball knowledge to understand this film.... almost everything now lives and dies on algorithms now... maybe if you only watched it on or around release by the statistical and data analytics boom in every sector though. 

But then you say the Big Short, also a classic.
  - Also a Brit. Love Moneyball, don't think the sport ultimately matters.
3. “Pi” and “Margin Call” come to mind
4. Great film. I would add in The Big Short, The Imitation Game, and Margin Call.
5. Big Short, Margin Call and Moneyball

## 4. What did you guys think about Casino (1995)

**Score:** 167
**Body:** I didn't mind the length. A crime classic. Go check my review on Letterboxd https://boxd.it/d3ow5h give it a like pls!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. love it
2. Great movie. Unfairly criticized as being the same as Goodfellas. The only thing in common is that it’s got the mob. 

Deniro and Pesci play different type of gangsters. Nicky is different from Tommy and Ace is not at all like Jimmy. 

I saw this in the theater and was mesmerized in the first hour or so. Scorcese’s description of the skim and Vegas is really well done. 

This is an underrated movie.
  - 'Underrated' and in this same thread 'best'. This movie is so good it should be in everyone's top ten. Until then, it is underrated.
3. Classic Scorsese movie is classic
4. Loved it. Not as good as Goodfellas by a hair, but entertaining. Guilty pleasure moment is watching Pesce beat Don Rickels up with a phone lol.
  - Goodfellas is definitely better.But both are good.
5. One of the best Gangster Movies of all time... Can't rate it better or worse than  Goodfellas.. They go hand in hand.. Like Lock Stock and Snatch
6. Only one of the greatest movies of all time.  I named my cat after Joe Pesci.
7. The town will never be the same. After the Tangiers, the big corporations took it all over. Today it looks like Disneyland. And while the kids play cardboard pirates, Mommy and Daddy drop the house payments and Junior's college money on the poker slots. In the old days, dealers knew your name, what you drank, what you played. Today, it's like checking into an airport. And if you order room service, you're lucky if you get it by Thursday. Today, it's all gone. You get a whale show up with $4 million in a suitcase, and some 25-year-old hotel school kid is gonna want his Social Security Number. After the Teamsters got knocked out of the box, the corporations tore down practically every one of the old casinos. And where did the money come from to rebuild the pyramids? Junk bonds. But in the end, I wound up right back where I started. I could still pick winners, and I could still make money for all kinds of people back home. And why mess up a good thing? And that's that.
8. Top ten greatest movies ever made.

## 5. Is this Nic Cage’s most underrated movie?

**Score:** 146

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Love this movie. Phoenix as max California was great. Nic Cage dialing it up. The end with Machine "What'd you expect? A monster?". Just a normal guy that was a psycho and liked to do fucked stuff
  - Don’t forget about James Galdolfini
2. The film is real
  - I got a friend who worked at blockbuster to rent this to me when I was 14. My dad saw the tape and drove it back to the store and chewed him out. Watched it years later and I understood why.
3. Got released around the same time as The Matrix.  I remember seeing both at the cinema a short time between each other.  Couldn't stop raving about the Matrix on the drive home, it was so exciting and ground breaking att ......and then the opposite drive home after seeing 8mm. Literally so shocked and disturbed that it left us speechless on the drive home. Could only talk about it after we got home.  Excellent movie which accomplished a masterpiece of shock, suspense and yes, definitely an underrated performance by NC
4. Bringing out the Dead
5. machine's most underrated movie
6. Amazing movie but hard to watch but always thought it a great cringy drama very underated
7. People who know, know, but I don't hear enough about Adaptation anymore.
8. No, that honour belongs to Adaptation



---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. What Have You Been Watching? (Week of (February 08, 2026)

**Score:** 11
**Body:**  Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything. Check out the [WHYBW archives](https://www.reddit.com/r/TrueFilm/wiki/index#wiki_what_have_you_been_watching.3F).

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. **A New Love In Tokyo (1994)**

I'd not noticed who the director of this was. (I put it on my watchlist based purely on the title/cover combo apparently) but in hindsight it makes total sense that this was a Banmei Takahashi film. Love his slasher Door (1988) to pieces!

This is kind of a sex-work found family type of movie?  What I really appreciatied about it was how "normal" everything was handled. I also really wanted the two main characters, the professional dominatrix and the call girl respectively, to end up with each other. They were very cute.

**Predator (1987)**

Watched this at a PACKED cinema screening and had a blast of a time. Everything comes together really well for this action flick: the ensemble cast is all on the same level and manage to carve out properly distinct characters in what limited screen time they manage to get, the tension is ratcheted up really nicely up to the point when you finally see the titular predator and I highly enjoyed the prey/predator reversal in the second half. 

**Civil War (2024)**

I had a completely different impression of this film apparently. Didn't have any inkling this was a coming-of-age story at all (interesting comparisons to Alex Garlands more recent 28 Years outings!). Also didn't know it would be about journalist. 

I quite liked the technique they did with the photographs and especiall how they managed to differentiate the kid's photos from the more seasoned photographers. There's an interesting overlap to A New Love In Tokyo, which utilitzes a similar visual device where the main character is photographed in her daily life in more or less intimate moments.

Jesse Plemmons really goes had in that five minute stretch that he's in.

Very cool. Didn't like the ending though.

**The Settlers (2023)**

This was visually really breath taking and story-wise very heavy. I thought the main actors really brought their A-Game. I know there's been some division about this film, also to do with how rape-y it is, but personally I found the approach very grounded. Horrific, yes. But never played for titillation. 

If there's one thing I didn't like, it's the time skip toward the third act. I didn't really vibe with that.

Assault on Precinct 13 (1976)

I watched that mostly because I've been on my bi-annual Supernatural Season 1-5 Rewatch and finally wanted to get the Jus In Bello reference haha

There was a surprising amount of built-up to the actual titular Assault on Precint 13 which I found interesting. Good villain work in that first half. 

The music is probably the standout here for me. Really good. 

Though I also loved the chemistry among the caged-in folks in the Precinct.




2. **Dhurandhar** — Fairly generic gangster action film with a spy twist that is all the rage here in India but I don’t get the hype. The plot is predictable to a fault and the action is the “cut on every hit” type that I don’t really care for. The gangsters and terrorists get the same hero shot that the hero gets so I’m not sure what the movie is trying to say there. The music is good. The performances are good. The production design is great.

**Scarlet** — As breathtaking and epic as it is messy. For some reason it kept reminding me Vision of Escaflowne. It’s such an interesting take on the portal fantasy trope though I guess they’re called isekai now. The world building is wonderful.

**Send Help** — This was a near perfect movie for what it’s trying to be. It’s got more on its mind than just a story about an exploited worker showing up her boss. It’s meaner, more cynical and a lot of fun. 

- - -

**Steal** — Kind of a stupid show, actually. It was engaging while I watched it but I couldn’t tell you anything now beyond the premise. 

**Earth Abides** — Interesting post apocalyptic series that doesn’t do a lot of the usual tropes. I just wish it had a protagonist who wasn’t a charisma vacuum and an ending that wasn’t so saccharine.

**Wonder Man** — This turned out to be so good. Acting is one of those art forms that’s hard to explain and often comes across as easy except for the flashy “eat a bison liver” kind. This show actually manages to convey what goes into crafting a performance. I loved the scene in the second episode where Trevor suggests Simon find what’s not being spoken and then Simon actually does it.
3. I watched Seven Samurai with a friend last night. It felt like I was watching a totem of action cinema. My favorite of the 7 was Kyoko. The only real hangup I had was the ending. Why did the head Samurai say they lost at the end of the battle?
  - As I recall his final dialogue is that the true victory belongs to the people who live in the village, which I think is just sort of a bittersweet acknowledgment of the somewhat transactional relationship they had with the villagers and the losses they experienced while protecting the village.
4. > Please don't downvote opinions. Only downvote comments that don't contribute anything.

That is *supposed* to be how all of Reddit works. What does it mean that the reminder is needed on this post specifically?
5. **The Call of the Cumberlands (1916, dir. Frank Lloyd)**

Sometimes when I watch an old film that's lived long enough to deteriorate, I get a little disappointed or maybe even annoyed that something is slowly marching towards unwatchability. When The Call of the Cumberlands started to fall apart, I was distraught. The cinematography in this movie is spectacular, and so many of its early shots are immediately spellbinding. But the candle that burns twice as bright lasts half as long, and when the film deteriorates, it really goes. It looks like it was exposed to radiation, and you feel helpless as you watch it because you know the film looks that good. I don't want to let this detract from how good the film does look, though. It's gorgeously shot, and perhaps one of the best-looking pre-color films.

Frankly, the filmmaking is too good for the story. The plot is meandering and clunky, taking influence from a few films from yesteryear and putting together a story that operates on "and then" storytelling more than anything else. It's strangely bisected, and neither half - one dealing with a family feud in rural Kentucky and the other with a convoluted conspiracy between some rich brats - is very interesting or well-developed on its own. The cinematography is what tides over the viewer, or what they can see of it.

**The Big Short (2015, dir. Adam McKay)**

The film is so full of jargon that even with comical fourth-wall asides I can't pretend to understand why or how exactly the Great Recession happened, which I think is a good thing. I like how McKay really branched out from goofy comedies and committed to something that's witty and consistently funny but is still so confident in the complex minutiae of financial skullduggery. It could have gone more of a The Office route where things are more accessible, but McKay doesn't treat the viewer like an idiot and values realism, which is commendable. I do prefer other modern biopic/historical dramas like The Social Network that have a little more narrative heft, but I can't say much bad about The Big Short.

**10 Cloverfield Lane (2016, dir. Dan Trachtenberg)**

A mostly effective sci-fi thriller picture. I think after Misery, you could only mine so much out of the "someone is nursing me back to health but they might be crazy!" premise, so I appreciate how quickly paced 10 Cloverfield Lane is. Trachtenberg knows that you know that John Goodman isn't totally trustworthy, so he frontloads the film with lots of subterfuge and questions about what's really happening outside, which is effective. When you have a clearer picture of what John Goodman is like with the mask off, it makes the scenes of quiet domesticity all the more tense than if he just started out genially.

The movie proceeds nicely until the ending. You could have cut out the last fivish, tenish minutes and left viewers off with an ambiguous ending. Is the world up shit creek? Was John Goodman full of shit? Who cares, Mary Elizabeth Winstead is okay and that's what matters. It was abundantly obvious that no one had intended this to be a sci-fi action movie, and the fight against the Combine slug from Half-Life 2: Episode 2 is the very definition of tacked on. However, it's entertaining on the whole and I don't think it's thematically inconsistent with the rest of the movie. At the start of the film, Winstead leaves her fiance and declines to actually talk to him, preferring evasion over confrontation. By the end, she has no choice but to confront another, more harmful man directly, but uses indirect methods to actually stop him. She prefers to kick a barrel of acid at Goodman and let him melt than to stab him. He ultimately dies due to an explosion that, while precipitated by Winstead, required little input from her. But with the monster mouth in the spaceship, she has to take more charge and make a Molotov cocktail, therefore completing her arc squarely. It's a nice touch that helps make the climax feel a little more earned, but tonally it is still at odds with the rest of the movie. The teaser at the end where she decides to continue the fight in Houston is pretty obnoxious, though.

**Christine (1983, dir. John Carpenter)**

Though I'm a Constant Reader, I've never read Christine. What interested me about this movie was Carpenter, not King. However, I was pleased to get a very Stephen King movie out of this. The characters and how they related to the supernatural conceit were very well done. I especially like Arnie, who's very Walter White in how it's clear that he's the cause of his own misery and the sudden external factor is incidental. The commentary on masculinity and how it's expressed by individuals and their trappings was very compelling and felt pretty modern.

And then the movie turns into a slow-burn slasher with a magic car. Perhaps that was always what it was. I like the second half, but it's a lot more uneven than the first half. Where it lacks for interesting character drama, it makes it up with entertaining car stuff and good special effects. The ending drags, though. Much like 10 Cloverfield Lane, the movie could have ended when Arnie's arc ended.

**Twinless (2025, dir. James Sweeney)**

Twinless takes the liar reveal plot structure, which can get a pretty bad wrap, and turns out a very effective film about grief and what we expect of people. Its characters do some pretty immoral things, but they're so sympathetic and their interactions are so emotionally complex that it's easy to reserve judgement until the credits roll. Sweeney and O'Brien's performances are very strong, and Sweeney's direction is very eye-catching. His use of color evokes Easter pastels and neon city lights at once, and I really got a kick out of the use of splitscreen. I don't know if Twinless overall reinvents the wheel, but it has a strong script and a strong style, and that's ultimately what mattered for the film.

**AVP: Aliens vs. Predator - Requiem (2007, dir. The Brothers Strause)**

The Brothers Strause... That should tell you everything.

AVP2's reputation precedes the film itself. I was told to expect something dark - not thematically, but literally dark, so fucking black I can't see the frame. In this instance, the film did not disappoint. I would say that this was the only way it didn't disappoint, but I wasn't expecting anything competent at all, which was indeed the case. It's no surprise that this movie killed both Alien and Predator for a short while. I get that the budget was a restraint, but the best The Brothers Strause could do was a cheap slasher movie? Narratively, neither Alien or Predator was revolutionary, but they were always a good step above this kind of schlock. Occasionally, there'll be an entertaining kill but the movie is too nihilistic to watch ironically. It's as entertaining as a mud puddle. That's actually probably where The Brothers Strause got their visual aesthetic from, too.

**Civilization (1916, dir. Reginald Barker)**

Civilization is a piano with one key, and Barker plays it like he thinks he's Frederic Chopin. Civilization is about war, and there's no mistaking its position. Nothing less than Jesus Christ repeating his own death can express Barker's opinions about the horrors of war. I hold anti-war sympathies, and I don't really disagree with most of what Civilization is saying, but it's so heavy-handed and single-minded that I come away disliking the movie. I think it's obnoxious and wastes my time. The story is poorly written and does nothing but prop up one position that it repeats *ad nauseum*. It's not entertaining or interesting. The framing doesn't help. The film argues that war is the domain of the uncivilized and that higher societies need to move past this. This colonialist mindset was prevalent in the early twentieth century, and was weaponized against the Germans during the First World War. They were seen in Anglophone propaganda as an aberrant great power that would extinguish the cultures of the world in favor of its anticultural anarchy. The German Empire did terrible things in WWI, but this anticulture mission is total bunk. Civilization is built on this terrible assertion.

None of this is to say that Civilization is poorly made from a filmmaking perspective, though. There are many battle sequences that are hectic but never nonsensical, and a well-made ship-sinking scene (an obvious reference to 1915's *Lusitania* disaster, which is in just horrible taste). The stand out sequence is when Jesus leads the King of Wrepryd, the man who started the war, through the battlefields and the aftermath of his soldiers' carnage. The destruction is harrowing, well-shot, and occasionally emotionally stirring. In terms of craft, Barker is at the top of his game, but the story itself is so preachy a cadre of priests is telling it to tone it down.
6. [**WEEK # 266:**](https://tilbageidanmark.tumblr.com/tagged/movies)

There are [12,502 films from Argentina logged on Letterboxd](https://letterboxd.com/films/country/argentina/by/rating/), and the 6th highest-rated feature film on the list is the unusually-modernist romantic comedy **SIDEWALLS** from 2011.

2 lonely and neurotic individuals who suffer from various forms of social anxiety, live across the street from each other, but don't hook up until the very last "Where's Wally"-inspired scene. It's a cliched story, but it is told in a uniquely original style, [where the every-day architecture of Buenos Aires plays the lead role.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WLBVo-cm6Yg) Except for a few misfired scenes where the director loses his train of thought, it's a perfectly visual delight for introverts.

(One gripe: Much is being made of the fact that they both live in tiny, cramped one-bedroom "shoe-box" apartments, but as the story progresses, these spaces fill up with more and more square meters, and in the end, they appear so much larger.)

[The trailer.](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=jF5alXD18pQ) **Recommended! 8/10.**

🍿

José Luis Guerín is an intellectual Spanish art-house director, known for his meditative 'Cinema as observation' style; Patient, urban and minimalist.

**IN THE CITY OF SYLVIA** (2007) is another drama starring the beautiful Argentinian actress Pilar López de Ayala (from 'Sidewalls' above). It's nearly a masterpiece, which reminded me of Michael Haneke's 'Caché', as different as it was from it. It has some deep flows which made it 'odd', but the parts of it that worked were genial. Nearly wordless, it tells of a romantic young artist who is looking for a woman he had a brief conversation with 6 years prior, and in the process follows (and stalks) a woman in an endless walk through the old city of Strasbourg. It's hypnotic and mysterious, and 'very' voyeuristic, nearly creepy. The sound editing especially stands out. 7/10.

*I'm off to see his 'Train of Shadows' and 'Work in Progress' next!*

🍿

**2 JAPANESE FILMS:**

🍿 **EARLY SUMMER** (1951) is my 7th serene ensemble piece by Yasujirō Ozu. Not only is it my favorite Ozu film, but it easily and immediately became **ONE OF MY TOP 10 FILMS EVER.** Maybe it's because I watched it stoned, but I was literally crying at how beautiful it played. So poetically simple, so magical in its description of a happy family. Absolutely magnificent. **10/10.**

💯 score on Rotten Tomatoes.

The final drone shot was memorable, but the 2 hours before it were dull and Hallmark'y. The cloths the farmers wore were spotlessly clean, and the main plot-line centered around Mr. Otani, the cheese-maker. 2/10.

**ROMANCE ON THE HIGH SEA** (1948) was sweet Doris Day's debut in a headliner role. As Ted Gioia assessed, she was a ["perfect leading lady for repressed libidos" of the time.](https://www.honest-broker.com/p/im-confessing-that-i-love-doris-day)

The story was laughable: A wealthy couple suspect that the other spouse cheats, so they both set up elaborate traps for each other, and the wife uses lounge singer Doris Day to pretend she's her, while on a cruise to Rio de Janeiro. First time that 'It's Magic' was used on screen. 4/10.

First watch: The Joan Crawford classic **MILDRED PIERCE** (1945), my 3rd Curtiz melodrama. It's a mixed up genre film, with murder, naked ambitions and greed, done in German expressionism style, which confused me. It deals with some unusual topics: Traditional family values but with a 2nd marriage, two divorces, a dead daughter and a dead husband. An independent woman entrepreneur, a working single mother to a "rotten little tramp" spoiled daughter, class conscience but targeted at the shame of working for a living at a blue color type of a job (waitressing, and pie baking, but even owning a restaurant chain is frowned upon). It also features Jack Carson, a distinctive sharp jaw dressed in large-shoulder suits. All in all, a slight disappointment I couldn't fully enjoy.

## 2. The Ignored Symbolism in Sabrina 1954

**Score:** 10
**Body:** I find it kind of surprising how no fan or blog post analyzing Sabrina ever names the key symbolism underlying the plot and characters. I appreciate this film for its character arcs and richly character-driven symbolism.

First let’s talk about Sabrina herself. Now, I think we can all agree that at the beginning of the film, her love for David Larrabee is all consuming, so much so that she is driven to suicide by carbon monoxide inhalation in the garage over his lack of affection for her. This is important because as she breathes in the fumes, they become too much for her, and she starts coughing, prompting her to open a window to make the process more bearable. A few moments later, Linus Larrabee shows up, and in response to her coughing says “You’ve gotta get out of here,” pulls her out and tells her to “breathe deep.” He then carries her up the stairs to her room since she passes out. This is symbolic of the entire plot of the film, where her love for David stifles her, and Linus is the one to break the spell. This is further developed when she goes to Paris, when the wealthy Baron says “Why try to get over it? You speak of love like it was a bad cough,” and when she dances with David at the Larrabee party, a servant says about David, “He’s holding her so close I don’t see how the poor girl can breathe!” Now in context, these lines are said in a positive way, but if you really analyze them, they tie back to the earlier garage scene beat for beat, and ultimately show that the hold David has on her emotions is suffocating. Linus being the person who “gets her out of there” so to speak is underscored by Sabrina’s statement at the end of the film before she boards the boat to Paris: “Dear David. Yes, I did get over that. I’m cured. Now how to get over the cure?,” referring to Linus. It is such an interesting phrase because it’s a callback to her “bad cough” from the garage that he helped her get over, and she can finally breathe. I know this is not really about her personal arc of immaturity to maturity, but it is her love for each brother that shows this arc. Her love for David keeps her in her childhood, but her newfound love for Linus allows her to fully reveal and acknowledge her maturity that she acquired in Paris.

Now onto Linus himself. His character I feel has the most detailed symbolism. There are four main symbols attached to his character: the egg, the plastic, his Homburg, briefcase, and umbrella, and sailboats. Let’s start with the egg, which I think is the most revealing symbol. In Paris, Sabrina’s cooking instructor teaches the class, “An egg is not a stone. It is not made of wood. It is a living thing, it has a heart. So when we crack it, we must not torment it. We must be merciful.” At the end, she easily cracks an egg in front of Linus in his office kitchen. He is the egg itself: a protective shell concealing a tender heart, and she cracks open his shell, revealing his heart. The plastic is a similar idea. Linus touts the product to David as “the greatest plastic ever made, not a scratch on it,” and it “doesn’t burn, doesn’t scorch, doesn’t melt.” It withstands bullets and tremendous weight. He even puts all the secretaries on it and David himself since he wants David to “see how resilient it is.” This is the front that Linus projects to the people in the Larrabee world. Solid, impervious, unaffected by any obstacle. He is the oldest son, the dutiful one who runs Larrabee Industries and corrals David who doesn’t work and is constantly in and out of romantic escapades that use him for his money. But when Linus instructs David to taste the plastic, David notes that it is sweet, since it’s made of sugarcane. By eye, the plastic seems indestructible, but by taste, it is sweet, just as Linus is himself if you get to know him in his most raw state. A camera trick that signifies this is when David is lying in his plastic hammock, and the camera pans behind it and captures Linus’s face behind the plastic as it moves back and forth. This causes the image of his face to be distorted by the plastic, indicating that this impenetrable exterior he projects is a distorted image of who he actually is. The connection between Linus and the plastic is complete at the end of the film, when David says “Linus Larrabee: the man who doesn’t burn, doesn’t scorch, doesn’t melt, suddenly throws a 20 million dollar deal out the window.” David is literally connecting the qualities of the plastic with Linus himself and showing that it is not an accurate depiction.

Linus’s Homburg, briefcase, and umbrella, serve as the articles of clothing that confer this impervious appearance. As Sabrina remarks, “As a child I used to watch you…always with your black Homburg, and carrying a briefcase and an umbrella. I thought you could never belong to anyone, never care for anyone.” But Linus reveals that this is not the case, as a certain song brings up past heartbreak, and he reveals that he contemplated suicide after a different breakup. This is precisely what draws Sabrina to him, since she attempted suicide as well, and he stopped her (even though he didn’t know it). She now sees who he really his, and this is why she wants him to go to Paris, since it is what helped her out of that situation, and she wants the same for him, since he was a contributing factor in helping her get to Paris in the first place (since she would have killed herself in the garage and never gone if it weren’t for his intervention). She turns down his hatbrim and tells him to abandon his umbrella and briefcase in Paris since she knows they make him look a certain way that isn’t accurate, and in Paris, one must be free of all constraints in order to be happy. David also serves as a foil in the final boardroom scene where he impersonates Linus by wearing his hat and swinging his umbrella. He uses the same tricks Linus used to get him with Elizabeth Tyson to ensure he gets on the boat with Sabrina. It is in this scene that everyone (the board and others) see Linus clearly, because he is stripped of his modes of defense (David is using them against him).

The umbrella is the most important item because umbrellas shield people from the rain, but as Sabrina says in her opening narration, “It never rained on the night of the Larrabee party. The Larrabees would never have stood for it.” Linus, as the son who must shoulder the Larrabee family’s interests, carries his umbrella to and from work, warding off the rain, performing the balancing act of keeping everyone and everything intact. But this comes at a cost, since it disguises his true self. Sabrina later tells Linus that when in Paris, he needs to “get himself some rain…the rain is very important because that’s when Paris smells its sweetest. It’s the damp chestnut trees.” Paris, the city of love, allows rain, which therefore allows sweetness to be perceived. The environment that Linus is in now prevents his sweetness from being perceived. This is why she insists “never an umbrella in Paris, and under all circumstances rain the very first day.” She wants him to let go and be free.

Finally, sailboats symbolize Linus’s longing to be free, and to be known, cared for, and loved. In the family photo shown at the beginning of the film where he was young, a model sailboat can be seen behind him. In his office, there is a model ship identical to the ship he and Sabrina leave on for Paris. In his office bedroom, there is a sailboat photo about the bed. Most importantly, he reveals his tragic romantic past to Sabrina when they are on a sailboat, the place where he is most open and raw. In fact, the name of the ship that takes them to Paris at the end is called the Liberté, which means “freedom” in French, a metaphor for how love freed them both from their constraints: Sabrina from her suffocating love for David, and Linus from the suffocating Larrabee world that concealed him. It is also fitting that Linus gives up his umbrella on the Liberté, since it shows he is finally allowing himself to be free now that he has someone who loves him.

There’s so much more to it than what I’ve written here, and I’d be happy to talk about it more if anyone is interested.

## 3. A Review of 'The Masque of the Red Death' (1964)

**Score:** 6
**Body:** In one of the many '60s gothic horrors directed by Roger Corman, 'The Masque of the Red Death'—a particular pinnacle of Price's entries that sustain a prolonged note of baseness and vice—his performance as Prince Prospero is an uneasy exercise in demonstrating how he can flit between noisome and puckish and make that felt to the audience, despite being a cruel and malevolent aristocrat who shies away from a plague and carouses in his debauched castle whilst the indigent citizens at the altar of his princedom are left to perish without a thought of benignancy on Prospero's part.

Yet, as we witness his attempts to stave off death and subject his nobles to humiliating feats of fealty, there is somehow a spark of inexplicable charm and magnetism that emanates from Price's trademark pencil moustache and preened airs; likely the conviction with which Prospero speaks in tandem with the choices of silence that are punctuated with his smiles and devil-may-care snickering. That propensity to almost always have us root for him in some capacity is the rare signifier of an actor who can actually turn the conventions of a story on its head and manipulate us along with his victims and fools.

Price at his pinnacle. This film exemplifies how best to employ Vincent Price and his enchanting presence. 'The Masque of the Red Death' is not only the greatest collaboration between director Roger Corman and the aforementioned muse, but also Corman's most memorable entry into the annals of film history and his truest adaptation of Edgar Allan Poe's work. The debauchery and comical villainy of Price's performance as Prince Prospero is utterly beguiling. Impish and delectably viral, I find that this film is the consummate Price Experience on screen. That is without aptly eulogising the wondrous use of colour, framing, symbolism, and the decade's experimentation.

## 4. The Manchurian Candidate (2004) - Needs a lot more love!

**Score:** 4
**Body:** This is a fantastic thriller and doesn’t get the praise it deserves, most likely because fans of the original saw it as blasphemy.  
  
I urge people to give it another chance. Jonathan Demme was one of our greatest directors, this guy gave us The Silence Of The Lambs and this superb thriller is the closest he got to that vibe before he was taken too soon. All his trademarks are there - actors looking right down the lens for that unparalleled empathy, an uncanny ability to creep out the audience when necessary, perfect pacing, great use of music, his usual company of supporting actors.  
  
Denzel is *on fire* here, he’s playing a much more vulnerable character and he really looks like a guy who’s clinging on to sanity by his fingertips. When your supporting cast includes Meryl Streep, Liev Schreiber, John Voight and Bruno Ganz then you’re in good company.  
  
Demme’s long time cinematographer Tak Fujimoto repeats his Lambs trick of flitting between realism and highly expressive imagery when needed, it’s a great film to look at.  
  
I generally loathe remakes but I think 40+ years and a shift from black & white to colour is enough distance for someone to try a fresh take. If you’ve dismissed this one then please give it another shot, and if you’ve never seen either and just want to see Denzel stretch himself then you’re in for a treat. Hollywood basically doesn’t make mature thrillers like this anymore.

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Eye roll. This breathless fan letter masquerading as criticism represents everything wrong with online film discourse. You provide zero substantive analysis of the screenplay's considerable weaknesses, avoids discussing how Demme's stylistic signatures serve this particular narrative, and refuses to engage with the film's updated politics. Instead, we get a laundry list of famous names, as if Meryl Streep's mere presence constitutes an argument. The claim that the film "doesn't get the praise it deserves" rings hollow given that critics and audiences largely dismissed it upon release because of its narrative incoherence and mistaken equation of baroque paranoia with genuine depth.

The defensive posture throughout reveals your awareness that conventional wisdom judges the film harshly. Yet your piece offers nothing beyond auteurist hand-waving about "Demme's trademarks" and the tired cliché about Hollywood abandoning mature thrillers. Genuine film criticism would grapple with the muddled second act, examine whether Streep's performance fits the film's tone, and analyze how Demme's intimacy techniques interact with the material. A real critical argument would address these elements directly and build a case for the film's merits despite apparent flaws.  Your "review" settles for "Denzel is on fire" and "great use of music." Freshman film students produce more penetrating analyses than this collection of promotional talking points dressed up with film school vocabulary.
  - Well now that you’ve appointed yourself the arbiter of correct criticism show me how it’s done. Let’s see your review of the film featuring ‘genuine film criticism‘…

## 5. I would like to discuss the movie If I Had Legs I Would Kick You.

**Score:** 2
**Body:** Please consider all of this spoiler from here on. If you read anything else from this point on, it is your own fault, because this is going to spoil the movie *If I Had Legs I Would Kick You*

SPOILERS BELOW

-----.---__---.;-0-.;.--.-.---:;----.-------_-

Okay, very little of the events in this movie actually happened literally. The entire movie is a reflection of a person going through a traumatic grief of some sort. What that grief is up to you, the viewer. what is unfolding before you is "real" Because your patient is telling you so. And to help your patient find their way to self help, you need to go through the events they tell you. Because the viewer, whether they like it or not, are playing the role of the Therapist listening to their patient.

No matter how nonsensical, or unrealistic; the events happening on screen need to be
viewed as something that could literally be happening in real time. you need to accept what your patient perceives as a core truth, before you can evaluate them.

I don't believe Linda (the viewer's patient) actually has a daughter. I believe she fell in love very young, got pregnant, and decided to not go through with it. A completely understandable decision, that she was most likely shamed for by the man, that got her pregnant.

Linda talks to her "therapist" played by Conan O'Brien. He is barely listening to her, dismissing her and trying to end their session unceremoniously. Linda then urges Conan to listen to the dream she had, but not the same dream she emailed him about the night prior, a new dream.

This is the first sign that this is not a literal reality.
After a back and forth on the beneficial elements of speaking about your dreams with another person that lives outside of your personal reality; Linda's therapist,Conan O'Brien relents because the dream was about him.

Linda tells a subjectively boring description of a dream we have all heard before. And its a little awkward because it kind of just sounds like Linda was thinking of YOU sexually. Dispite her saying nothing of the sort.

She then leaves this office, into a hallway that is white with thickly painted white canvas'. This leads to her office where she is met by a young man wearing a shirt that says Fuck Fuck Fuck, and he recites a dream he had to Linda that ends with him and her kissing, mirroring the conversation with Conan O'Brien.

These two interactions represent Linda in her youth. When her life was a blank canvas. Conan is her relationship "future", her patient with the Fuck shirt is her relationship "past".

She is then seen arguing with her husband Captain Christian Slater on the phone, but you only hear his voice. This is her relationship "present". Cpt Christian Slater, Conan O'Brien, and Caroline's husband are all the same person through different stages of Linda's partner, that got her pregnant in the real world.

A$AP Rocky and everyone at the hotel are what if scenarios. There are two characters who have no face. A man sitting in the corner of the open hallway kiddy corner from Linda's room at the Hotel, and a faceless friend of the Hotel front desk worker. When she is getting denied alcohol.

These are the friends/unliked peers she could have met had she not "settled down".

She brings A$AP Rocky to witness the hole in the ceiling, and he doesn't even notice it at first. Instead he comments on the room being covered in plastic. Once he is told to look at the hole, he says it's not as bad as Linda thinks it is, looking closer, he begins to tell her how he can help her repair this hole.

Linda has a rough memory of her daughter being restrained by doctors as they insert a tube into the daughters belly. But the daughter is only a extension of Linda. The hole in her ceiling, and the hole in her daughter are one in the same, and the tube is a means of self reflection and a funnel for therapeutic practices, that Linda thinks are bullshit.

Linda explains she had a second pregnancy to Conan, well a first pregnancy, but she terminated this pregnancy, and she says that it hurt, and she didn't realize how much it would hurt.

Then a doctor explains that there is no surgery to take the tube out of Linda's daughter. The doctor can just pull it out, this begins the existential breakdown of our patient, Linda.

Linda screams, and A$AP Rocky falls down the hole in the apartment breaking his leg. We instantly cut to Linda at work, not even thinking of that happening

We see Linda finding and losing Caroline, pulling out her daughters tube, running back home, finding a work crew at night going in and out of her home. And her husband Captain Christian Slater is home seemingly fixing the hole that was made in the home that she was estranged from. 

When A$AP was there, at that same "home", he told Linda she could have just slept in another room, instead of going to the hotel. but Linda chose to go to the hotel anyway.

Linda and Cpt. Christian Slater go back to the hotel room where they find A$AP Rocky in the hotel room, where Linda calls him the babysitter, A$Ap is having none of that. And Cpt Christian Slater gets extremely defensive. Ultimately ending up in Cpt Christian Slater discovering that the tube has been removed from his daughter.

This represents the complete detachment from any man romantically, as Linda runs to the same beach that Caroline ran to. Trying to ascend into the beyond, but the waves keep pushing Linda back to the shore. She sees the same dust hallucination, screams of the past, and golden darkness.

Linda then sees her daughter on the beach waking her up. This is the first time you see the daughter's face. She doesn't look sickly. She made it to the beach by herself. In prior scenes she couldn't go anywhere without her mom there as a her crutch. A crutch A$AP Rocky refused to use.

Linda is not speaking to her daughter here. She never had a daughter. She is forgiving herself for what she viewed as an unsuccessful relationship with a man that treated her like a burden. This is shown through Christian Slater, Conan and Caroline's husband.

Linda tells her daughter "I will do better".

This movie is annoyingly beautiful, and extremely metaphorical.

This is a story about self-love self-hate addiction family mental health personal friendship and social acceptance all at the same time. What are your thoughts? I really want to discuss this movie.
Do you like it personality?

1. Commenting again because apparently my first comment wasn’t long enough. 

This is an inappropriately tortured analysis. Read the interviews with the director, who talks about her own experiences as a mother of an ailing child (she plays the doctor who’s always scolding Rose Byrne for her failure to attend group sessions). Talk to any parent who has had to juggle work, a sick child, and even one more tiny thing. 

The film is actually a rigorously honest portrayal of what we ask of mothers and little support they get. 

I did think the choice not to show the daughter as a whole person rather than close-up body parts until the end was brilliant: this is not her story.
